editableGoldMemories.label: Thoughts/Curiosity
editableGoldMemories.value: Repeatedly asks for grammar/punctuation help and wants edits clearly marked (e.g., highlighted/underlined) when revising text like CV extracts.
editableGoldMemories.evidence_text: Can you check the grammar and punctuation for me and correct anything needed... / Is there anything you would change in this text? / Now I will add part of my CV would you also be able to help fix any issues with this please? / Can you highlight the parts that you changed... / Can you just underline any changes to the original please

editableNewDialogue.text: Quick grammar check please—does this sound okay: “We’ll meet on Tuesday, and then we can finalise the agenda; any feedback is welcome.”

It’s already clear. A slightly smoother version would be: “We’ll meet on Tuesday, then we can finalise the agenda. Any feedback is welcome.” (Using a full stop instead of a semicolon makes it a bit cleaner.)
Not wrong—just optional. A semicolon is fine when linking two closely related independent clauses. In your sentence, a full stop (or an em dash) is more typical for a simple message.
Got it. Also, what’s the difference between “finalise” and “finalize”?
Same meaning. “Finalise” is more common in British/Australian English; “finalize” is more common in American English.
Random question: I saw a headline about a library digitising old maps—how do they avoid damaging them?

Typically with non-contact or low-pressure scanning (overhead scanners), controlled lighting to reduce heat/UV exposure, and supports/cradles so bindings aren’t forced flat. Conservators may also do minor repairs first if paper is fragile.
Quick one before I send it off: for the CV bit, should I keep “educationalist” in there, or swap it for something like “educator”? I don’t want it to sound odd in UK English.
“Educator” is generally the safer, more widely used choice in UK English. “Educationalist” can sound a bit academic or niche, so unless you’re aiming for that tone, I’d switch to “educator.”

Quick correction for the record: that CV snippet was from a few years back. I’m not working as a Customer Service Agent or an English Teacher now—I’m currently between roles and just reusing old experience for applications.

Got it—so those titles reflect past experience in your CV, not your current occupation. Since you’re between roles at the moment, we shouldn’t treat “Customer Service Agent & English Teacher” as your present job.
